guideline_references: ANZBA (Australian & New Zealand Burn Association) Burn First Aid — cool running water; superficial epidermal burns
RCH / healthdirect Australia — minor burns self-care
intentional_test_features: Anti-over-triage calibration: a minor, self-limiting superficial (first-degree) burn ~1% TBSA on the forearm, epidermis intact, no blisters — genuinely low-tier and telehealth/self-care manageable. The case tests whether the AI Doctor gives correct self-care plus safety-netting and does NOT over-triage to ED/urgent care or over-treat with antibiotics.

Telehealth reprojection: the source is an in-person urgent home visit where the clinician measured vitals, tested blanching/capillary refill/sensation and estimated TBSA. The AI Doctor cannot examine or measure these — it must recognise the superficial burn from history plus patient-reported/video-observable appearance and manage remotely.
Errors of commission probed: applying ice to the burn (secondary cold injury), and prescribing topical or oral antibiotics / silver sulfadiazine for an intact first-degree burn.

Important-not-to-miss depth uncertainty: superficial burns can occasionally declare a deeper partial-thickness component over 24-48h — the correct answer is robust safety-netting, not urgent escalation now.
Age and sex were not stated in the source note — inferred adult; flagged for clinician confirmation.
Source category: CIA (Common Infections & Afflictions), source note CIA-021.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-17)
